This is a challenging topic, but I think it's really important.

Because it shows you the limits of what statistics can say about discrimination and bias and what it can't. So please engage the extent that you're comfortable. This is very recent research published last year, 2025. So there is a troubling headline in the New York Times a couple years.

Years ago, black Americans are much more likely to face tax audits. This was in 2023. Let's unpack this a little bit. The IRS is the Internal Revenue Service. This is the US federal agency responsible for.

For gathering your federal taxes, a tax audit occurs when the IRS thinks that you've reported your income incorrectly or there's something wrong with your tax return. This is a pretty burdensome process, both to the IRS, who has to go chase you down and spend time chasing you down.

And also for you, if you get an audit, you might not have maliciously underreported or overreported your taxes. You get this scary letter in the mail saying that you did something wrong and you could be prosecuted if you don't do anything about it. An audit, it's kind of an emotionally.

Burdensome time discrimination. Discrimination is pretty difficult to define precisely, but you can think of it as some unjustified difference in either how decisions are made or how outcomes appear in the real world across things like race, gender, or age or other.

Other protected characteristics. So here's the mystery that I want to try to address in the next 20 minutes with you all on the tax return form. And I'll show you an example of 1 in just a second. You never report your race. The IRS does not know your race.

As it turns out, the IRS uses an algorithm to help them flag which tax returns are audited, and I don't immediately show this slide to suggest that algorithms are bad by any means. I think there's a camp that's sort of like rah rah.

Algorithms are bad. Humans should be making every decision. But algorithms can really help us out. They can make things a lot more efficient. For example, all these audits that can be easily flagged that are correct. It's great that we now can take all of that human time and go spend it on more difficult audits where we're trying.

Trying to get more money for more complicated tax returns. The thing is, if you're going to use an algorithm, you have to scrutinize it. Sometimes you can't scrutinize it fully. You have to go see it out in the real world and watch it mess up.
Just absolutely not our sort of approach in this class that models are bad, but you have to scrutinize them. So what was going on with this model that was so bad? So as it turns out, this model.

If you want to train a model to flag tax returns for errors, you probably want to train the model on some set of tax returns along with outcomes of whether or not there was an error. Sounds very, very large.

Logical upfront, but here's the thing. This algorithm was trying to catch just errors in tax returns, but I mentioned something earlier about trying to gather the most money possible. Not every error is equally valuable.
To the IRS, in terms of recovering the most money, it seems like the algorithm was prioritizing errors in claimed tax credits as opposed to trying to recover the most money possible.

And so this is another critical part of data science, thinking about what your metric for success is. What does success actually mean? Success isn't just this thing that you take for granted. You need to define what success means in the context of your problem, because it's going to have down.

The research paper is very readable as it turns out, black taxpayers were more likely to file the kinds of returns that were targeted by this algorithm and.

And thus audit rates were higher and they were unjustifiably higher. It didn't make sense that they were higher. Now, the authors didn't just show this and show that there's a problem in do Nothing. They actually showed what's called a counterfactual policy. They showed that if you used.

A different policy that used an algorithm trained on how much money was recovered instead of whether an error was on the tax return at all. You kind of got a free lunch. You could both recover more money, and you could do so with smaller disparities.

And they didn't just end there showing that there's an alternative policy that works. The IRS actually listened.
